text: Your organization consumes order data from a third-party logistics vendor via a \textbf{REST API}. The API requires an \textbf{OAuth 2.0 Bearer token} in the request header and returns paginated JSON responses. Each page contains up to 200 records, and the response body includes a \texttt{nextPageUrl} field that is \texttt{null} when no further pages exist. You need to load all order records into \textbf{Power BI Desktop} using the \textbf{Web connector} with a custom function that handles pagination. Which approach correctly implements this requirement?

options.A: Use \textbf{Get Data} $\rightarrow$ \textbf{Web}, enter the base API URL, and in the \textbf{Advanced} tab add an \texttt{Authorization} header with the Bearer token value. Then in \textbf{Power Query Editor}, write a recursive \texttt{List.Generate()} function that calls \texttt{Web.Contents()} on each successive \texttt{nextPageUrl} until the field returns \texttt{null}, combine all page results with \texttt{Table.Combine()}, and invoke the function as a new query.
options.B: Use \textbf{Get Data} $\rightarrow$ \textbf{OData Feed}, enter the base API URL, and select \textbf{Organizational account} as the authentication method. Power BI will automatically detect the \texttt{nextPageUrl} field and handle pagination natively without any custom M code.
options.C: Use \textbf{Get Data} $\rightarrow$ \textbf{Web}, enter the base API URL with the Bearer token appended as a query string parameter. Use \texttt{Table.ExpandListColumn()} on the JSON response to flatten all pages simultaneously, since Power BI automatically follows JSON continuation links.
options.D: Use \textbf{Get Data} $\rightarrow$ \textbf{Blank Query} and write a single \texttt{Json.Document(Web.Contents())} call. Set the \texttt{RelativePath} option on \texttt{Web.Contents()} to the Bearer token value so that Power BI authenticates and retrieves all pages in one request.

explanation: \textbf{Option A — CORRECT:} The \textbf{Web connector's Advanced mode} supports adding custom HTTP headers, including the \texttt{Authorization: Bearer \{token\}} header required by \textbf{OAuth 2.0} APIs. Because REST APIs do not natively surface pagination to Power BI, a custom \textbf{M language} function using \texttt{List.Generate()} is the standard pattern for iterative pagination: it repeatedly calls \texttt{Web.Contents()} with each successive URL until \texttt{nextPageUrl} is \texttt{null}, collects results into a list, and \texttt{Table.Combine()} merges all page tables into a single dataset. This is the correct, professional approach for paginated REST APIs in Power BI.\\[6pt] \textbf{Option B — INCORRECT:} The \textbf{OData Feed connector} is designed specifically for OData protocol endpoints (which use \texttt{\$skiptoken} or \texttt{@odata.nextLink} conventions). A generic REST API returning a custom \texttt{nextPageUrl} field is not an OData feed. Additionally, \textbf{Organizational account} authentication uses Azure AD credentials, not a static Bearer token, so this option addresses neither the authentication nor the pagination requirement correctly.\\[6pt] \textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} Embedding a Bearer token as a query string parameter is a security anti-pattern and is not supported by most OAuth 2.0 implementations, which require the token in the HTTP \texttt{Authorization} header. Furthermore, Power BI does \textit{not} automatically follow JSON continuation links; pagination must be handled explicitly in M code. \texttt{Table.ExpandListColumn()} expands nested lists within an existing table but does not fetch additional API pages.\\[6pt] \textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} The \texttt{RelativePath} option in \texttt{Web.Contents()} is used to append a path segment to a base URL (enabling query folding and avoiding \textit{Formula.Firewall} errors), not for passing authentication tokens. Placing a Bearer token in \texttt{RelativePath} would produce a malformed URL and fail authentication. A single \texttt{Web.Contents()} call also cannot retrieve multiple pages in one request.

text: Your organization exposes an internal REST API that returns paginated JSON responses. Each response includes a \textbf{nextLink} property containing the URL for the next page of results. When all pages have been retrieved, the \textbf{nextLink} property is \textbf{absent} from the final response. You are building a solution in \textbf{Power Query} (\textbf{Power BI Desktop}) to retrieve all pages of data automatically. Which approach correctly implements recursive pagination for this scenario?

options.A: Use the \textbf{Web.Contents} function inside a \textit{List.Generate} expression, where the initial state fetches the first page, the next-state function follows the \textbf{nextLink} URL, and the condition function checks whether the parsed JSON response contains a \textbf{nextLink} field — terminating the loop when it is absent.
options.B: Enable the \textbf{Relative paths} option in \textbf{Web.Contents} and pass each page number as a query parameter using \textbf{Query} in the options record; Power Query will automatically detect the end of pagination when an empty response is returned.
options.C: Use the \textbf{OData.Feed} connector and set the \textbf{Implementation} option to \texttt{"2.0"} — this causes Power Query to automatically follow server-driven paging via \textbf{nextLink} regardless of whether the API conforms to the OData protocol.
options.D: Wrap \textbf{Web.Contents} in a \textbf{Table.FromList} call, passing a pre-built list of all page URLs. Then use \textbf{Table.ExpandRecordColumn} to merge all pages. Power Query's \textit{query folding} engine will detect the \textbf{nextLink} pattern and stop fetching automatically.

explanation: \textbf{Option A — CORRECT:} \textbf{List.Generate} is the idiomatic \textbf{Power Query} approach for server-driven pagination. It accepts three functions: an \textit{initial} function (fetch and parse the first page), a \textit{condition} function (return \texttt{true} while the current state's parsed response contains a \textbf{nextLink} field), and a \textit{next} function (call \textbf{Web.Contents} with the \textbf{nextLink} URL and parse the result). When the final page is returned without \textbf{nextLink}, the condition evaluates to \texttt{false} and the loop terminates. The resulting list of response records is then combined with \textbf{List.Transform} and \textbf{Table.Combine}. This fully handles dynamic, server-controlled pagination without knowing the total page count in advance. \\[6pt] \textbf{Option B — INCORRECT:} The \textbf{Relative paths} and \textbf{Query} options in \textbf{Web.Contents} are used to satisfy \textit{gateway compatibility} and credential caching (so the base URL remains static). They do not implement automatic pagination logic; the developer must still write explicit iteration. \\[6pt] \textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} \textbf{OData.Feed} does automatically follow \textbf{\$skiptoken} or \textbf{nextLink} for genuine \textit{OData} feeds. However, the question explicitly states this is a proprietary REST API — not an OData service. Using \textbf{OData.Feed} against a non-OData endpoint will either fail or produce malformed results. The \textbf{Implementation} option controls OData protocol version compliance, not generic REST pagination. \\[6pt] \textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} \textbf{Table.FromList} with pre-built URLs requires knowing all page URLs in advance, which is impossible with server-driven \textbf{nextLink} pagination. Furthermore, \textit{query folding} applies to relational data sources (e.g., SQL) and has no relevance to REST API pagination detection.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Web, OData, REST API Connectors

text: Your organization exposes an internal REST API that returns paginated JSON data. Each API response includes a \textbf{nextLink} property containing the URL for the next page of results. The final page has no \textbf{nextLink} property. You need to connect to this API in \textbf{Power BI Desktop} and retrieve all pages of data into a single table using \textbf{Power Query}. Which approach correctly implements this requirement?

options.A: Use the \textbf{Web connector} with a static URL and enable the \textit{Relative Path} option in the connector dialog. Power Query will automatically detect and follow \textbf{nextLink} pagination without additional configuration.
options.B: Use the \textbf{OData connector}, which natively handles \textbf{nextLink}-based pagination. Point the connector at the API base URL and Power Query will automatically merge all pages into a single result set.
options.C: Use the \textbf{Web connector} to call the first page URL, then write a recursive \textbf{Power Query M} function using \texttt{List.Generate} that calls each successive URL from the \textbf{nextLink} property until the property is absent, and finally combine all page results using \texttt{Table.Combine}.
options.D: Use the \textbf{Web connector} and configure the \textit{Advanced} query parameter to append \texttt{\$expand=nextLink} to the base URL. Power Query will then automatically paginate through all result sets before loading data into the model.

explanation:
\textbf{Option C — CORRECT:} When a REST API uses \textbf{nextLink}-based pagination, Power Query has no built-in automatic pagination mechanism for arbitrary REST APIs. The correct approach is to use the \textbf{Web connector} to fetch the first page and then write a custom \textbf{M} function using \texttt{List.Generate}. \texttt{List.Generate} accepts an initial state (the first URL), a condition (\textbf{nextLink} is not null), a transformation (parse the JSON body), and a next-state generator (extract the \textbf{nextLink} URL). Once all pages are collected as a list of records, \texttt{Table.Combine} merges them into one flat table. This is the standard, exam-tested pattern for custom REST API pagination in Power Query.

\textbf{Option A — FALSE:} The \textbf{Web connector} does not have a \textit{Relative Path} option that auto-detects or follows \textbf{nextLink} pagination. There is no such automatic detection for arbitrary REST APIs.

\textbf{Option B — FALSE:} The \textbf{OData connector} does natively follow \texttt{\$skiptoken} or \texttt{odata.nextLink} pagination, but only when the endpoint fully conforms to the \textit{OData protocol specification}. A generic REST API that merely returns a \textbf{nextLink} property is not an OData-compliant feed, so the OData connector cannot be used.

\textbf{Option D — FALSE:} \texttt{\$expand} is an OData query option used to expand related entities, not a mechanism for pagination. Appending \texttt{\$expand=nextLink} is semantically incorrect and will not cause Power Query to paginate automatically through any API.

text: You are designing a Power BI solution for a retail company with a 12 GB product catalog database. The source data is updated daily at 2 AM and contains product details, pricing, and inventory levels. Business users need to perform interactive filtering and slicing by product category, region, and time period. Data freshness requirements allow for a 24-hour delay. Which storage mode should you recommend?

options.A: \textbf{Import Mode} — Load the entire dataset into Power BI's semantic model, compress it in-memory, and schedule a daily refresh to align with source updates.
options.B: \textbf{DirectQuery} — Connect live to the source database to guarantee real-time data and avoid storing large datasets in Power BI.
options.C: \textbf{Live Connection} — Use Live Connection to connect directly to the relational database for always-current data access.
options.D: \textbf{Composite Model} — Combine Import and DirectQuery modes to balance performance and data currency across multiple tables.

explanation:
extbf{Statement 1 — Requirement Analysis:} The scenario specifies three key constraints: (1) 12 GB source database, (2) daily update cadence at 2 AM, (3) 24-hour data freshness acceptable, and (4) interactive filtering/slicing required.

	extbf{Statement 2 — Option A Evaluation (TRUE):} 	extbf{Import Mode} is the optimal choice because: (i) A 12 GB source dataset compresses significantly in Power BI's in-memory engine. After compression, it typically reduces to 2–4 GB within Power BI Premium capacity limits (P1 and above support up to 400 GB per semantic model). The source size does not equal the compressed model size; (ii) A single daily scheduled refresh at 2 AM aligns perfectly with the source update window and satisfies the 24-hour freshness requirement; (iii) Import Mode delivers superior interactive performance for filtering and slicing operations because all data is cached in-memory, eliminating query round-trips to the source; (iv) No query latency — filters and cross-filtering respond immediately. This is the best practice for scenarios with acceptable data latency and strong performance requirements.

	extbf{Statement 3 — Option B Evaluation (FALSE):} 	extbf{DirectQuery} has two critical drawbacks in this scenario: (i) Query latency — Every filter interaction, slicer change, or cross-filter generates a query to the source database. With complex multi-dimensional filtering (category, region, time), query response time becomes noticeable and degrades user experience; (ii) Source system load — DirectQuery places continuous query load on the production database, increasing resource contention and operational risk; (iii) Performance trade-off — While DirectQuery guarantees real-time data, this is unnecessary here because 24-hour freshness is acceptable. DirectQuery sacrifices interactive performance for a freshness benefit that is not required.

	extbf{Statement 4 — Option C Evaluation (FALSE):} 	extbf{Live Connection} is used exclusively to connect Power BI to pre-built analytical models such as 	extbf{Analysis Services (SSAS)}, 	extbf{Azure Analysis Services}, or published 	extbf{Power BI semantic models}. It does NOT connect directly to relational transactional databases like product catalogs. Live Connection cannot be used against the retail company's source database in this scenario.

	extbf{Statement 5 — Option D Evaluation (FALSE):} 	extbf{Composite Models} mix Import and DirectQuery tables within a single semantic model. This adds operational complexity without benefit in this scenario. The product catalog is a single, unified dataset with a uniform refresh requirement. Splitting it into Import and DirectQuery portions would complicate refresh logic, model maintenance, and relationships without addressing any actual requirement.

	extbf{Conclusion:} 	extbf{Import Mode} with a daily scheduled refresh is the best-practice choice because it delivers fast interactive performance, aligns refresh cadence with source updates, respects the acceptable data latency window, and minimizes database load on the production source system.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Import Mode vs DirectQuery vs Live Connection – Trade-offs & Use Cases

text: You are designing a Power BI solution for a financial organization with a large transactional database containing 180 million records. The organization requires that reports always display the most current data without any manual refresh, and historical snapshots must be minimized to reduce storage requirements. Which data connectivity mode should you recommend?

options.A: \textbf{Import Mode} — loads all data into the Power BI model, enabling fast queries and offline access
options.B: \textbf{DirectQuery} — queries the source database directly, ensuring real-time data and eliminating the need to store copies in Power BI
options.C: \textbf{Live Connection} — establishes a direct link to Analysis Services (SSAS) or a Power BI semantic model, preventing custom model creation
options.D: \textbf{Composite Model} — combines Import and DirectQuery tables within the same model, allowing mixed connectivity strategies per table

explanation:
\textbf{Correct Answer — B: DirectQuery} is the appropriate choice for this scenario. DirectQuery is optimal when:

• The data source is extremely large (180 million records makes Import Mode storage-prohibitive, especially when storage constraints are a priority)

• Real-time data freshness is mandatory (no scheduled refresh delays; queries execute against the live source)

• Storage footprint must be minimized (DirectQuery does not replicate data into Power BI; only metadata and query results are cached)

• The organization prioritizes data currency over query latency within acceptable bounds

\textbf{Statement A — FALSE:} \textit{Import Mode} would require replicating 180 million records into the Power BI dataset, consuming substantial storage and still requiring scheduled refreshes to maintain currency. This directly contradicts the storage-minimization and real-time freshness requirements.

\textbf{Statement C — FALSE:} \textit{Live Connection} connects to Analysis Services (SSAS) instances or Power BI semantic models—not directly to raw relational source tables. While it provides real-time connectivity to SSAS or semantic models, it does not support direct connections to SQL Server tables and restricts DAX modeling. For this scenario requiring direct access to a large transactional database with minimal storage, Live Connection is not the appropriate choice.

\textbf{Statement D — FALSE:} \textit{Composite Model} allows individual tables to be configured as Import or DirectQuery within one model. While useful for medium-scale scenarios requiring mixed connectivity patterns, it does not solve the core requirement: for a 180-million-row transactional table requiring real-time updates, pure DirectQuery eliminates redundant storage and guarantees freshness more effectively.

\textbf{Trade-off Summary:} DirectQuery sacrifices some query speed due to source database latency but guarantees real-time accuracy and eliminates the need to store large data copies in Power BI. For this organization, minimized storage footprint and current data take priority over sub-second query performance.

text:
You are building a \textbf{Power BI} report for a retail organization. The dataset contains 2.8 million transaction records from a SQL Server database updated daily at 2:00 AM. Business users require fast, interactive dashboard performance for sales analysis and reporting. Network bandwidth to the data source is limited, and the organization does not have \textbf{Analysis Services} or \textbf{Power BI Premium} infrastructure in place.

Which connection mode best balances query performance, bandwidth constraints, and refresh requirements in this scenario?

options.A: \textbf{Import Mode} — copies all 2.8 million rows into the Power BI dataset at 2:00 AM daily, enabling fast local queries and minimal network bandwidth consumption during interactive analysis.
options.B: \textbf{DirectQuery} — sends each user query to the SQL Server database in real-time, ensuring current data but consuming significant bandwidth and potentially causing performance delays during peak usage.
options.C: \textbf{Live Connection} — establishes a persistent link to Analysis Services, delivering optimized queries without importing data into Power BI.
options.D: \textbf{Dual Mode} — combines Import Mode on dimension tables with DirectQuery on fact tables to optimize query paths and reduce dataset size.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Import Mode vs DirectQuery vs Live Connection – Trade-offs & Use Cases

text: You are designing a Power BI solution for a retail company with 3.5 million customer transaction records stored in an Azure SQL Database. The dataset is updated every 15 minutes, and business users need to perform ad-hoc analysis on real-time sales trends. However, the IT department has strict bandwidth limitations and wants to minimize data transfer from the database to Power BI Desktop. Which data connectivity mode should you recommend, and why?

options.A: \textbf{Import Mode} — Import the entire dataset once daily to reduce bandwidth usage and maximize query performance.
options.B: \textbf{DirectQuery} — Query the database directly to ensure real-time data refresh within the 15-minute update window while minimizing data transfer to Power BI.
options.C: \textbf{Live Connection} — Use Live Connection to maintain a persistent session with the database and automatically refresh all visuals every 5 minutes.
options.D: \textbf{Dual Mode} — Import summary tables and use DirectQuery for detailed transaction records to balance performance and real-time requirements.

explanation:
\textbf{Analysis:} This scenario requires balancing three competing priorities: real-time data freshness (15-minute updates), bandwidth constraints, and large dataset size (3.5 million records).

\textbf{Statement A — FALSE:} Import Mode would violate bandwidth constraints by transferring millions of rows daily and cannot support real-time 15-minute refresh cycles efficiently. Daily imports are too infrequent.

\textbf{Statement B — TRUE:} \textbf{DirectQuery} is the optimal choice because:
• Query aggregations occur at the source database (no full dataset transfer).
• Satisfies real-time requirements — queries execute against current database state.
• Minimal bandwidth impact — only aggregated results and query responses cross the network.
• Aligns with IT's strict bandwidth policy.
• Business users get current sales trends without data transfer overhead.

\textbf{Statement C — FALSE:} \textbf{Live Connection} (typically for Analysis Services/Power BI Premium) would not reduce bandwidth constraints — it maintains persistent connections that may consume more resources than DirectQuery for this use case.

\textbf{Statement D — FALSE:} While \textbf{Dual Mode} (Composite) can be useful, it requires careful partitioning strategy and adds complexity. For a real-time requirement and bandwidth constraint, DirectQuery alone addresses both concerns more directly.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Import Mode vs DirectQuery vs Live Connection – Trade-offs & Use Cases

text:
A retail analytics team is designing a Power BI solution to address three distinct reporting needs. You must recommend the most appropriate connectivity mode for each scenario.

\textbf{Scenario A:} An inventory tracking dashboard that must display near-real-time stock levels from a large Azure Synapse Analytics table containing 400 million rows. Business users require data that is no more than a few minutes old, and the dataset is far too large to load into memory.

\textbf{Scenario B:} A quarterly sales summary report built from 1.8 GB of cleansed, historical transaction data extracted from a data warehouse. The data is static between refreshes, and the report must support complex DAX time-intelligence calculations with fast response times for 60 end users.

\textbf{Scenario C:} A regional performance report for store managers that connects to a centralized, pre-built \textbf{Analysis Services} model maintained by the BI team. The store managers should always see the official, governed metrics without the report developer needing to replicate the model logic.

Which combination of connectivity modes is most appropriate?

options.A: Scenario A: \textit{Import}; Scenario B: \textit{DirectQuery}; Scenario C: \textit{Live Connection}
options.B: Scenario A: \textit{DirectQuery}; Scenario B: \textit{Import}; Scenario C: \textit{Live Connection}
options.C: Scenario A: \textit{DirectQuery}; Scenario B: \textit{Live Connection}; Scenario C: \textit{Import}
options.D: Scenario A: \textit{Live Connection}; Scenario B: \textit{Import}; Scenario C: \textit{DirectQuery}

explanation:
\textbf{Analysis of Optimal Connectivity Modes:}

\textbf{Scenario A — DirectQuery (CORRECT):} The dataset contains 400 million rows, making \textit{Import} mode impractical due to the prohibitive memory footprint and refresh duration. \textit{Live Connection} requires an existing \textbf{Analysis Services} or \textbf{Power BI semantic model} — it cannot connect directly to Azure Synapse Analytics tables. \textbf{DirectQuery} is the correct choice: queries are sent directly to Azure Synapse Analytics at report interaction time, ensuring data currency of only a few minutes without loading data into Power BI's memory. Azure Synapse is purpose-built for large-scale query workloads, making it a suitable DirectQuery source.

\textbf{Scenario B — Import (CORRECT):} With a static 1.8 GB dataset, monthly or quarterly refresh cycles, and a requirement for complex \textbf{DAX time-intelligence} calculations, \textit{Import} mode is optimal. Data is compressed and loaded into Power BI's in-memory VertiPaq engine, enabling fast query response for all 60 users without repeatedly hitting the source system. \textit{DirectQuery} would degrade DAX time-intelligence performance and add unnecessary source load for static data; \textit{Live Connection} requires a pre-existing centralized model that is not described here.

\textbf{Scenario C — Live Connection (CORRECT):} \textbf{Live Connection} is specifically designed to connect a Power BI report to an existing, pre-built \textbf{Analysis Services} (on-premises or Azure) model or a published \textbf{Power BI Premium semantic model}. It allows report authors to leverage official, governed metrics and hierarchies without duplicating or reimplementing the model logic. All calculations remain in the centralized model, ensuring consistency. This is the canonical use case for Live Connection.

\textbf{Why other options fail:}

\textbf{Option A} assigns \textit{Import} to Scenario A — importing 400 million rows exceeds practical memory constraints and defeats the near-real-time requirement.

\textbf{Option C} assigns \textit{Live Connection} to Scenario B — Live Connection requires a pre-existing \textbf{Analysis Services} or semantic model; it cannot connect to a raw data warehouse table or support custom model development.

\textbf{Option D} assigns \textit{DirectQuery} to Scenario C — DirectQuery connects directly to data sources and does not leverage a pre-built \textbf{Analysis Services} model, losing the governance and consistency benefits the scenario requires.

text: A retail company has a 	extbf{Power BI} report connected to an 	extbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} dedicated SQL pool. The dataset contains aggregated sales data totaling roughly 80 GB. The analytics team requires that executives always see 	extit{real-time} inventory levels when they open the report, with no tolerance for stale data. However, the report also includes a complex 	extbf{DAX} calculation that aggregates three years of historical transactions, and this calculation performs poorly when issued as a live query against the warehouse. Which connectivity mode and approach 	extbf{best} balances real-time freshness for inventory with acceptable performance for the historical DAX calculation?

options.A: Use \textbf{DirectQuery} for all tables so that every visual always reflects the latest data in the warehouse, accepting the performance trade-off for the complex DAX calculation.
options.B: Use \textbf{Import} mode for all tables and configure an hourly scheduled refresh so inventory data is never more than one hour old.
options.C: Use a \textbf{Composite Model} — set the inventory table to \textbf{DirectQuery} storage mode and import the historical aggregation table, allowing real-time inventory queries while caching the expensive historical calculation.
options.D: Use a \textbf{Live Connection} to an \textbf{Analysis Services} model hosted in Azure, which automatically separates real-time and cached data without any additional configuration.

explanation:
\textbf{Option C — Correct:} A \textbf{Composite Model} (mixed storage mode) is purpose-built for this scenario. By setting the inventory table to \textbf{DirectQuery} storage mode, every report interaction queries the warehouse directly, guaranteeing real-time freshness. The historical aggregation table is set to \textbf{Import} storage mode, so Power BI caches it in-memory and the complex \textbf{DAX} calculation executes against the fast VertiPaq engine rather than the warehouse, resolving the performance bottleneck. Composite Models became generally available in Power BI and are explicitly supported against Azure Synapse dedicated SQL pools.

\textbf{Option A — Incorrect:} Pure \textbf{DirectQuery} satisfies the real-time requirement but does nothing to address the poor performance of the complex DAX aggregation; every interaction still fires an expensive query against the warehouse.

\textbf{Option B — Incorrect:} \textbf{Import} mode with hourly refresh violates the stated requirement that executives always see \textit{real-time} inventory levels — data can be up to one hour stale.

\textbf{Option D — Incorrect:} A \textbf{Live Connection} to Analysis Services delegates all storage and calculation decisions to the remote model; Power BI Desktop cannot independently configure per-table storage modes through a Live Connection, so this does not give the report author the control needed to separate real-time and cached tables.

text: A retail company has a 	extbf{Power BI} report connected to a 	extbf{SQL Server} data warehouse. The warehouse contains a 	extbf{sales fact table} with approximately 	extbf{900 million rows} that grows by several million rows per hour. Business analysts require dashboards that always reflect data no older than 	extbf{five minutes}. The IT team has confirmed that the SQL Server instance can handle concurrent analytical queries efficiently. However, the company also has a 	extbf{product dimension table} with only 	extbf{4,000 rows} that changes 	extbf{once per quarter}. A junior developer proposes using 	extbf{Import mode} for all tables to maximize report performance. Which approach best meets the near-real-time requirement while optimizing overall solution design?

options.A: Use 	extbf{DirectQuery} for the sales fact table and 	extbf{Import mode} for the product dimension table, leveraging a 	extbf{composite model} to combine both modes in one dataset.
options.B: Use 	extbf{Import mode} for all tables and schedule 	extbf{incremental refresh} every five minutes to keep the data current.
options.C: Use 	extbf{DirectQuery} for all tables to ensure every visual always queries live data from SQL Server.
options.D: Use a 	extbf{Live Connection} to an 	extbf{Analysis Services} model that imports all data, and rely on the AS cache to serve near-real-time data.

explanation:
extbf{Option A — CORRECT:} A 	extbf{composite model} is the optimal solution here. 	extbf{DirectQuery} on the 900-million-row fact table ensures analysts always see data within seconds of it being written to SQL Server, satisfying the five-minute freshness requirement without needing to import hundreds of gigabytes. Simultaneously, placing the small, slowly-changing 	extbf{product dimension table} in 	extbf{Import mode} means dimension lookups are served from the in-memory cache, dramatically improving query performance for most report visuals that join sales to products. Power BI 	extit{composite models} explicitly support mixing Import and DirectQuery storage modes within the same dataset.

	extbf{Option B — INCORRECT:} 	extbf{Import mode} loads a snapshot of data into the Power BI dataset. Even with incremental refresh, the minimum practical scheduled refresh interval in the 	extbf{Power BI service} is typically around 	extbf{30 minutes} for most capacities (8 times per day on Pro, up to 48 times per day on Premium), and importing 900 million rows repeatedly would be impractical and resource-intensive. This approach cannot reliably guarantee five-minute data freshness.

	extbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} While using 	extbf{DirectQuery for all tables} would satisfy the freshness requirement, it forgoes the performance benefits of caching the small, static dimension table in memory. Every report interaction would issue live queries for both fact and dimension data, increasing query latency unnecessarily when a composite model would deliver better performance without sacrificing freshness on the fact table.

	extbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} A 	extbf{Live Connection} to 	extbf{Analysis Services} delegates all query processing to the AS model. If the AS model itself uses Import mode, data freshness depends on when that model was last processed, not on the underlying SQL Server. This does not solve the five-minute freshness requirement unless the AS model is also refreshed every five minutes, which reintroduces the same limitations as Option B at the AS layer.

text: You are designing a \textbf{Power BI} solution for a large financial services organization. The company maintains an \textbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} dedicated SQL pool containing 250 million transaction records updated hourly. Business analysts need real-time dashboards showing current trading positions, regulatory compliance metrics, and intraday profit-and-loss summaries. Simultaneously, the organization wants to enable self-service analytics on historical customer segmentation data (stored in \textbf{Azure SQL Database}, 5 million rows, updated weekly). Network bandwidth to the Synapse pool is constrained during peak hours. Most end users access reports via mobile devices with variable connectivity. Your team must minimize refresh times, ensure real-time data accuracy for compliance reports, and manage licensing costs effectively. Which combination of data connectivity modes and architectural decisions best addresses ALL of these requirements?

options.A: Use \textbf{Live Connection} to an \textbf{Azure Analysis Services} tabular model for trading and compliance data; use \textbf{Import Mode} for customer segmentation. Build two separate reports and use \textbf{Power BI Apps} to present a unified experience to analysts.
options.B: Use \textbf{DirectQuery} against \textbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} for trading and compliance dashboards; use \textbf{Import Mode} for the Azure SQL Database customer segmentation dataset (5M rows, weekly refresh). Combine both in a \textbf{Composite Model}, and add aggregation tables over the Synapse source to reduce query load during peak hours.
options.C: Use \textbf{Import Mode} for both sources with a 30-minute incremental refresh on the Synapse pool and a weekly scheduled refresh on customer segmentation. Apply \textbf{query folding} to push transformations back to both sources and use \textbf{aggregation tables} to reduce dataset size at refresh time.
options.D: Use \textbf{DirectQuery} for both sources to ensure real-time consistency across all reports. Avoid \textbf{Import Mode} entirely to eliminate bandwidth spikes caused by large scheduled refreshes, and rely solely on \textbf{query optimization} at the source to meet performance SLAs.

explanation:
\textbf{Statement 1 — ANALYSIS OF REQUIREMENTS:} The scenario presents competing constraints: (1) real-time compliance accuracy with intraday regulatory mandate requiring zero data staleness for trading data, (2) constrained peak-hour bandwidth to Synapse, (3) mobile access with variable connectivity, (4) large row volumes (250M) making full Import Mode imports impractical, and (5) heterogeneous refresh cadences (hourly vs.~weekly). This is a classic \textit{Composite Model} design problem.

\textbf{Statement 2 — WHY OPTION B IS CORRECT:} \textbf{DirectQuery} against \textbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} ensures trading position and compliance dashboards always reflect the current state of the database — zero staleness, satisfying the regulatory real-time mandate. The \textbf{Azure SQL Database} customer segmentation dataset (5M rows, weekly updates) is a textbook candidate for \textbf{Import Mode}: small relative size, infrequent updates, and fast query performance in-memory. A \textbf{Composite Model} blends both connectivity modes within a single \textbf{.pbix} file, enabling analysts to perform cross-source drill-throughs in one unified report. \textbf{Aggregation tables} pre-summarize the 250M-row Synapse data; Power BI routes summary-level queries to the in-memory aggregations and only issues DirectQuery requests for detail-level data, dramatically reducing peak-hour bandwidth consumption and improving mobile responsiveness.

\textbf{Statement 3 — WHY NOT OPTION A:} \textbf{Live Connection} is a valid mode but connects to a semantic layer (\textbf{Azure Analysis Services}, \textbf{SQL Server Analysis Services}, or an existing \textbf{Power BI semantic model}) — NOT directly to a relational database such as Azure Synapse Analytics or Azure SQL Database. Building a separate AAS tabular model adds infrastructure cost and complexity. Furthermore, Live Connection to a single AAS model prevents combining it with a separately imported dataset in one report without switching to a Composite Model, negating the stated unified-experience benefit. Two separate reports with Power BI Apps does not constitute a single integrated analytics solution.

\textbf{Statement 4 — WHY NOT OPTION C:} Importing 250M rows from \textbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} on a 30-minute cycle would generate enormous bandwidth spikes precisely during peak hours — directly violating the bandwidth constraint. Even with incremental refresh, the volume of changed rows each 30 minutes in a high-frequency trading environment can still be very large. Import mode introduces up to 30 minutes of staleness for compliance data, which is unacceptable under a real-time regulatory mandate. \textbf{Query folding} assists Import Mode transformations but does not resolve the fundamental staleness and bandwidth problems.

\textbf{Statement 5 — WHY NOT OPTION D:} Using \textbf{DirectQuery} for the Azure SQL Database customer segmentation dataset (5M rows, updated weekly) is suboptimal. Every analyst interaction issues a live query to the Azure SQL Database, increasing latency and source system load unnecessarily for data that changes only once per week. \textbf{Import Mode} is the superior and recommended choice for infrequently updated, moderately sized datasets. DirectQuery-only designs also prevent leveraging in-memory aggregations that are most effective when paired with \textit{Composite Models} combining Import and DirectQuery storage modes.

text:
You are designing a Power BI solution for a large retail organization with the following requirements:

\begin{itemize}
\item A fact table containing 847 million transaction records updated hourly in the data warehouse
\item 312 concurrent users accessing reports during peak hours
\item Real-time inventory levels must be displayed with maximum 90-second latency
\item Users frequently filter by product category, region, and date range
\item Storage limitations restrict the model size to 2.4 GB
\item Query performance must remain under 3 seconds for 95th percentile user interactions
\item The organization has invested in a high-performance SQL Server with columnstore indexes
\end{itemize}

Your technical team evaluates three statements:

\textbf{Statement 1:} Importing all 847 million rows into memory will violate the 2.4 GB storage constraint and create unacceptable hourly refresh latency.

\textbf{Statement 2:} A pure \textbf{DirectQuery} mode will guarantee sub-3-second query performance for all 312 concurrent users.

\textbf{Statement 3:} A \textbf{Composite Model} combining \textbf{DirectQuery} on the fact table with \textbf{Import mode aggregations} and \textbf{Dual mode} on dimensions can satisfy all stated requirements.

Which combination of statements is accurate?

options.A: Statements 1 and 2 are true; Statement 3 is false. Pure \textbf{DirectQuery} is always the optimal choice for real-time requirements with high concurrency.
options.B: Statements 1 and 3 are true; Statement 2 is false. A hybrid \textbf{Composite Model} with \textbf{DirectQuery} on detail rows and \textbf{Import mode aggregations} for summaries best satisfies all requirements.
options.C: Only Statement 1 is true; Statements 2 and 3 are false. \textbf{Import mode} with incremental refresh is the only viable solution given the storage constraint.
options.D: All three statements are true. A \textbf{Live Connection} to an Analysis Services model eliminates all storage, concurrency, and latency trade-offs simultaneously.

text:
A global retail organisation has a 	extbf{Power BI Premium} capacity. Their data engineering team maintains a certified, enterprise-wide 	extbf{Power BI dataset} (semantic model) in the 	extbf{Power BI service} that is refreshed every 30 minutes. Multiple report authors need to build departmental reports on top of this model. The Chief Data Officer has issued the following requirements:

\begin{itemize}
\item Report visuals must always reflect the latest available data without requiring manual refresh.
\item Report authors must not be able to modify the underlying data model schema (e.g., add measures to the source model).
\item The solution must minimise data duplication across the organisation.
\item Report authors should be able to add their own local measures inside their reports.
\end{itemize}

Which connectivity mode should report authors use when building their departmental reports?

options.A: \textbf{Import Mode} — each report author imports a full copy of the certified dataset into their own \texttt{.pbix} file and schedules independent refreshes.
options.B: \textbf{Live Connection} to the published \textbf{Power BI dataset} — report authors connect directly to the shared semantic model, can add local report-level measures, but cannot alter the source model schema.
options.C: \textbf{DirectQuery} against the underlying data warehouse — each report author bypasses the certified dataset and queries the source database directly for real-time data.
options.D: \textbf{Composite Model} — report authors switch the certified dataset connection to \textit{Dual} storage mode and add new tables from additional sources, publishing a new independent model.

explanation:
\textbf{Option B — CORRECT (Live Connection to a Power BI Dataset):} \textbf{Live Connection} to a published \textbf{Power BI dataset} in the \textbf{Power BI service} (also called a \textit{shared semantic model} or \textit{remote model}) satisfies all four requirements simultaneously. (1) Visuals always reflect the latest data because no local cache exists — queries are sent to the shared model, which is refreshed every 30 minutes and Power BI retrieves the most recently refreshed data. (2) Report authors cannot modify the source model schema; they are read-only consumers of it. (3) Data duplication is eliminated — a single certified dataset serves all reports. (4) Authors can still add \textbf{report-level measures} (local measures scoped to their \texttt{.pbix}) without touching the source model.\\[6pt]
\textbf{Option A — INCORRECT:} \textbf{Import Mode} requires each author to maintain their own refresh schedule, duplicates data across multiple \texttt{.pbix} files, and violates the no-duplication requirement. Authors could also accidentally alter or extend the imported model schema.\\[6pt]
\textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} Connecting via \textbf{DirectQuery} directly to the data warehouse bypasses the certified dataset entirely, undermines data governance, and ignores the requirement to build on top of the approved semantic model. It also introduces unnecessary load on the source system.\\[6pt]
\textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} Building a \textbf{Composite Model} on top of the certified dataset does allow adding new tables, but it creates a new independent model that duplicates and potentially diverges from the certified one, violating the no-duplication and schema-immutability requirements. Composite Models also require \textbf{Build} permission on the source dataset and introduce governance complexity the CDO sought to avoid.

text: You are building a 	extbf{composite model} in Power BI Desktop that combines sales data from an 	extbf{Azure SQL Database} (using 	extbf{DirectQuery}) with product dimension data from an 	extbf{Excel file} (using 	extbf{Import} mode). When you create a relationship between the two tables, Power BI displays a warning icon. What does this warning indicate?

options.A: The relationship is marked as 	extbf{limited}, meaning referential integrity is not assumed and bidirectional cross-filtering may be restricted.
options.B: Composite models require all tables to use the same storage mode; you must convert the Excel table to DirectQuery.
options.C: The 	extbf{DirectQuery} table and the 	extbf{Import} table have conflicting data types in their key columns.
options.D: The relationship cardinality must be restricted to many-to-one ($m:1$) only; many-to-many ($m:m$) relationships are not supported in composite models.

explanation:
extbf{Explanation:} In a 	extbf{composite model}, Power BI allows mixing 	extbf{Import} and 	extbf{DirectQuery} storage modes within the same data model. However, when a relationship spans across storage modes—connecting an 	extbf{Import} table to a 	extbf{DirectQuery} table—Power BI marks the relationship as 	extbf{limited}. A limited relationship indicates that: (1) referential integrity is not assumed between the tables, and (2) bidirectional cross-filtering behavior may be constrained. The warning alerts the designer to these behavioral implications.

	extbf{Statement B — FALSE:} Composite models explicitly support mixed storage modes; converting all tables to the same mode would defeat the purpose of selective use of Import and DirectQuery optimizations.

	extbf{Statement C — FALSE:} While data type mismatches can prevent relationship creation entirely, the warning specifically indicates a valid but limited relationship has been created, not a data type conflict.

	extbf{Statement D — FALSE:} Composite models support many-to-many ($m:m$) relationships across storage modes. The limitation is not cardinality-based but rather concerns referential integrity guarantees and cross-filtering directionality.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Composite Models – Mixing Import & DirectQuery

text: You are configuring a 	extbf{composite model} in Power BI Desktop that combines sales data from an 	extbf{Import} table and customer demographic data from a 	extbf{DirectQuery} source. You need to ensure optimal query performance while maintaining data freshness for the customer demographics. What is the primary advantage of using a composite model in this scenario instead of importing both tables?

options.A: The composite model automatically compresses all data, reducing file size by up to 90 percent regardless of source type.
options.B: The composite model allows the DirectQuery table to maintain real-time data freshness while the Import table provides fast local query performance for historical analysis.
options.C: The composite model eliminates the need to configure row-level security (RLS) on either table.
options.D: The composite model converts all DirectQuery sources to Import mode after the first refresh cycle to improve performance.

explanation:
extbf{Statement 1 — Correct Answer:} Option B correctly identifies the core advantage of 	extbf{composite models}. By combining 	extbf{Import} mode for the sales table and 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode for the customer demographics, you achieve a balanced approach: the Import table provides fast local query performance for historical sales data, while the DirectQuery table ensures customer demographic data remains current without requiring a full model refresh. This is the primary design purpose of composite models.

	extbf{Statement 2 — Option A (FALSE):} While composite models can optimize storage, they do not automatically guarantee 90 percent compression. Compression depends on data cardinality, redundancy, and column types. This is an incorrect generalization.

	extbf{Statement 3 — Option C (FALSE):} Composite models do not eliminate RLS requirements. RLS must still be configured separately for each table or source if row-level restrictions are needed. Composite models inherit the security context from both Import and DirectQuery sources.

	extbf{Statement 4 — Option D (FALSE):} This is fundamentally incorrect. Composite models do not convert DirectQuery tables to Import mode. The purpose of DirectQuery in a composite model is to maintain its query-against-source behavior; conversion would defeat the purpose of mixing storage modes.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Composite Models – Mixing Import & DirectQuery

text:
You are building a 	extbf{Power BI} report for a retail company. The data model includes a large fact table with 5 million rows of transaction history stored in 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}, and a smaller dimension table with 2,000 product categories stored in an on-premises 	extbf{SQL Server}. You decide to use a 	extbf{composite model} with mixed storage modes.

Which storage mode configuration would provide the best balance between query performance and data freshness when the transaction table must reflect near-real-time updates but the product dimension changes infrequently?

options.A: Set the fact table to 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode and the dimension table to 	extbf{Import} mode.
options.B: Set both the fact table and dimension table to 	extbf{Import} mode with scheduled refresh every hour.
options.C: Set the fact table to 	extbf{Import} mode and the dimension table to 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode.
options.D: Set both tables to 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode with query folding enabled on all transformations.

explanation:
extbf{Composite Models} in 	extbf{Power BI} allow you to mix 	extbf{Import} and 	extbf{DirectQuery} storage modes within a single data model, enabling optimization based on data characteristics and business requirements.

	extbf{Statement 1 — Correct Answer (Option A):} Setting the fact table to 	extbf{DirectQuery} ensures that queries against transaction data always reflect the most current values in 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}. Simultaneously, importing the 2,000-row dimension table is highly efficient and eliminates round-trips to the on-premises 	extbf{SQL Server} for every query. This configuration balances near-real-time accuracy for the high-volume fact table with fast dimension lookups.

	extbf{Statement 2 — Why Option B is incorrect:} While importing both tables simplifies the model, the hourly refresh cadence cannot guarantee near-real-time updates to transaction data. If updates occur between refresh cycles, users see stale data.

	extbf{Statement 3 — Why Option C is incorrect:} Placing the fact table in 	extbf{Import} mode defeats the goal of near-real-time transaction visibility. Additionally, using 	extbf{DirectQuery} for a small 2,000-row dimension table is inefficient—import mode is designed for such small, slowly-changing reference tables.

	extbf{Statement 4 — Why Option D is incorrect:} While 	extbf{DirectQuery} on both tables guarantees current data, it introduces unnecessary performance overhead for the dimension table query and increases latency on every interaction, especially when the dimension is already small and stable enough to be cached in memory.

Composite models unlock the ability to use 	extbf{DirectQuery} for large, rapidly-changing fact tables while using 	extbf{Import} for efficient dimension management, optimizing both refresh overhead and user experience.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Composite Models – Mixing Import & DirectQuery

text: You are building a \textbf{composite model} in Power BI that combines data from multiple sources. Your fact table containing sales transactions (18 million rows) is sourced from \textbf{Azure SQL Database} using \textbf{DirectQuery} mode. Your dimension table for product categories (523 rows) is imported from an \textbf{Excel file}. When you attempt to create a relationship between these tables, Power BI displays a warning about the relationship becoming \textbf{limited}. What is the primary reason for this warning?

options.A: Composite models do not support relationships between DirectQuery and Import tables; you must convert all tables to the same storage mode.
options.B: The relationship becomes a \textbf{limited relationship} because Power BI cannot assume \textbf{referential integrity} across different storage modes and sources, which may lead to unexpected query results.
options.C: DirectQuery tables cannot establish many-to-many relationships with Import tables; only one-to-many relationships are supported in composite models.
options.D: Import tables cannot participate in relationships with DirectQuery tables unless both tables are sourced from the same database connection.

explanation:
\textbf{Statement A — FALSE:} Composite models explicitly support relationships between \textbf{DirectQuery} and \textbf{Import} tables. This is a core feature designed to allow mixing of storage modes within a single semantic model.

\textbf{Statement B — TRUE:} When creating a relationship between a \textbf{DirectQuery} table and an \textbf{Import} table in a composite model, Power BI marks the relationship as \textbf{limited}. A \textbf{limited relationship} indicates that Power BI cannot assume \textbf{referential integrity} across different storage modes and data sources. This is the primary reason for the warning: because the two tables come from different sources (one queried live via SQL, one imported into the in-memory cache), Power BI cannot guarantee that every foreign key value in the DirectQuery table has a corresponding primary key in the Import table. This uncertainty affects how Power BI generates queries and may lead to unexpected filtering or aggregation behavior.

\textbf{Statement C — FALSE:} Composite models do support many-to-many relationships between DirectQuery and Import tables. Cardinality is not restricted to one-to-many; many-to-many relationships are valid in composite models.

\textbf{Statement D — FALSE:} Relationships between DirectQuery and Import tables do not require both tables to share the same database connection. They can originate from completely different data sources (Azure SQL Database, Excel, CSV, etc.). The limitation is about referential integrity assumptions, not data source connectivity.

text:
A Power BI developer is building a solution for a manufacturing company. The model includes the following data sources:

\textbf{Source 1}: A large on-premises SQL Server database containing three years of production order history (several hundred million rows, refreshed nightly)

\textbf{Source 2}: A cloud-based PostgreSQL database containing current machine sensor readings (updated every few minutes, relatively small volume)

The developer wants stakeholders to be able to cross-filter between historical production orders and live sensor readings in a single \textbf{Power BI Desktop} report, while keeping historical query performance fast and sensor data always current.

Which storage mode configuration should the developer apply to achieve this outcome?

options.A: Set both tables to \textbf{DirectQuery} mode so that all data is always live and cross-filtering is fully supported without any import overhead.
options.B: Set the production order history table to \textbf{Import Mode} and the sensor readings table to \textbf{DirectQuery Mode}, creating a \textbf{composite model} that allows cross-source relationships.
options.C: Set both tables to \textbf{Dual Mode} so that Power BI can decide at query time whether to use cached or live data for each table.
options.D: Connect to both sources using \textbf{Live Connection} so that Power BI can federate queries across both databases in real time.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Composite Models – Mixing Import & DirectQuery

text:
You are designing a 	extbf{Power BI} model for a retail company with the following requirements:

• Sales data: ~8 million rows, updated hourly via an automated ETL pipeline
• Product catalog: ~45,000 SKUs, updated monthly
• Customer dimension: ~620,000 records, updated daily
• Real-time inventory counts: queried from a live operational database

Your organization has limited Premium capacity and wants to optimize query performance and refresh time. You decide to use a 	extbf{composite model}.

Which configuration should you implement to meet these requirements while minimizing refresh time and memory consumption?

options.A: Import all tables (Sales, Products, Customers, Inventory) using scheduled refresh. Use 	extbf{DirectQuery} for none of them.
options.B: Use 	extbf{DirectQuery} for Sales and Inventory; 	extbf{Import} Products and Customers. Enable aggregations on the Sales table.
options.C: Use 	extbf{Import} for Sales, Products, and Customers; 	extbf{DirectQuery} for Inventory only. Configure query folding on all DirectQuery sources.
options.D: Use 	extbf{DirectQuery} for all tables to ensure real-time accuracy across all dimensions and minimize storage.

explanation:
\textbf{Analysis of the Scenario:}

The question tests understanding of 	extit{composite model} design decisions in Power BI, specifically the trade-off between query performance, refresh latency, and real-time requirements.

\textbf{Why Option C is Correct:}

1. \textbf{Sales (Import):} At ~8 million rows with hourly updates, importing Sales into the model cache allows fast aggregations and DAX calculations. Hourly refresh is manageable and improves report responsiveness.

2. \textbf{Products (Import):} 45,000 rows is well within memory budgets. Monthly updates mean infrequent refresh cycles—no performance penalty.

3. \textbf{Customers (Import):} 620,000 records is reasonable for import; daily refresh is lightweight and enables efficient joins in memory.

4. \textbf{Inventory (DirectQuery):} Real-time operational data must use \textbf{DirectQuery} to avoid stale snapshot issues. This is the only table requiring live access to the source database.

5. \textbf{Composite Model Benefit:} The model can filter imported dimensions (Products, Customers) and apply them against the DirectQuery Inventory table, reducing query load on the source system.

\textbf{Why Other Options are Incorrect:}

\textbf{Option A — FALSE:} All-import approach ignores real-time inventory requirement. Inventory snapshots would become stale between refresh cycles, causing inaccurate stock reporting.

\textbf{Option B — FALSE:} Importing Sales (8M rows, hourly) and using DirectQuery for Inventory is valid, but using DirectQuery for the high-volume Sales table creates unnecessary source system load. The 8M row import volume is acceptable, and hourly refresh is feasible. Aggregations alone do not justify the overhead of DirectQuery for this use case.

\textbf{Option D — FALSE:} All-DirectQuery defeats the purpose of Power BI's in-memory analytics. Query performance suffers, user interactivity degrades, and Premium capacity is underutilized. Dimensions (Products, Customers) especially benefit from import for fast joins and filters.

\textbf{Key Concept:} In composite models, use \textbf{Import} for relatively static dimensions and fact tables with acceptable refresh windows; use \textbf{DirectQuery} only for real-time requirements or extremely large, infrequently accessed tables.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Composite Models – Mixing Import & DirectQuery

text: Your organization maintains a 	extbf{Power BI} semantic model that uses a 	extbf{Composite Model}. The model connects to two data sources: a large 	extbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} table containing three years of historical sales transactions (imported into Power BI via 	extit{Import mode}), and a real-time 	extbf{Azure SQL Database} table showing live inventory levels (connected via 	extit{DirectQuery mode}). A report author wants to create a visual that combines 	extbf{SalesAmount} from the imported historical sales table with 	extbf{CurrentStock} from the live inventory table in a single matrix visual, filtered by 	extbf{ProductCategory}. Which statement 	extbf{best} describes how Power BI handles this cross-source relationship when the visual is rendered?

options.A: Power BI will refuse to render the visual because relationships between 	extbf{Import} and 	extbf{DirectQuery} tables are not supported in a 	extbf{Composite Model}.
options.B: Power BI will send a 	extbf{DirectQuery} to 	extbf{Azure SQL Database} for live inventory data and combine the results with the cached imported data in-memory on the 	extbf{Power BI engine}, applying filters from 	extbf{ProductCategory} across both sources.
options.C: Power BI will convert the 	extbf{DirectQuery} table to 	extbf{Import mode} at query time, caching live inventory data before merging it with historical sales data to render the visual.
options.D: Power BI will push all calculations to 	extbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} as a federated query, including the 	extbf{CurrentStock} column from the 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}.

explanation:
In a 	extbf{Composite Model}, Power BI allows relationships between tables sourced from different storage modes, including 	extbf{Import} and 	extbf{DirectQuery} tables. When a visual requires data from both sources simultaneously, the 	extbf{Power BI engine} orchestrates the query execution as follows:

\textbf{Option B — CORRECT:} Power BI issues a 	extbf{DirectQuery} to the live source (	extbf{Azure SQL Database}) to retrieve current inventory data, then merges those results with the pre-cached imported data from 	extbf{Azure Synapse Analytics} inside the in-memory 	extbf{VertiPaq engine}. Filters such as 	extbf{ProductCategory} are passed to both sources appropriately. This is the defining behavior of 	extbf{Composite Models} — enabling real-time and cached data to coexist in a single visual.

\textbf{Option A — INCORRECT:} Cross-source relationships between 	extbf{Import} and 	extbf{DirectQuery} tables are fully supported in 	extbf{Composite Models}. This is one of the primary use cases for the feature.

\textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} Power BI does 	extit{not} temporarily convert a 	extbf{DirectQuery} table to 	extbf{Import mode} at query time. The storage mode is a design-time setting and is not altered dynamically during visual rendering.

\textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} Power BI does not federate queries across unrelated engines (e.g., pushing 	extbf{Azure SQL Database} columns to 	extbf{Azure Synapse Analytics}). Each source is queried independently, and result merging occurs within the 	extbf{Power BI} service.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Composite Models – Mixing Import & DirectQuery

text: A large retail organisation has a 	extbf{Power BI} semantic model that currently uses 	extbf{Import mode} for all tables. The data engineering team informs you that a new 	extbf{real-time inventory table} has been added to an 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}; this table is updated every few minutes and must always reflect the latest stock levels in reports. However, the remaining dimension tables (products, stores, employees) are relatively static and total several hundred million rows — making full DirectQuery performance on them unacceptable. You need to redesign the model so that real-time inventory data is always live while maintaining fast query performance on the dimension tables. Which approach should you implement?

options.A: Convert all tables to 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode pointing to 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}, and use query folding to optimise performance on dimension tables.
options.B: Create a 	extbf{Composite Model} by keeping the dimension tables in 	extbf{Import mode} and setting only the real-time inventory table to 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode against 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}.
options.C: Keep all tables in 	extbf{Import mode} and schedule a 	extbf{dataset refresh} every 15 minutes to capture near-real-time inventory changes.
options.D: Use 	extbf{Dual storage mode} on the real-time inventory table so that 	extbf{Power BI} can cache the data locally while also querying it live when needed.

explanation:
extbf{Option B — CORRECT:} A 	extit{Composite Model} in 	extbf{Power BI} allows individual tables within the same semantic model to use different storage modes. By keeping the large, static dimension tables in 	extbf{Import mode}, queries against those tables benefit from the in-memory 	extbf{VertiPaq} engine, delivering fast performance even at hundreds of millions of rows. Simultaneously, setting the real-time inventory table to 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode ensures that every report interaction issues a live query to 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}, guaranteeing up-to-the-minute stock levels. This is the canonical use case for Composite Models and directly addresses both requirements.

	extbf{Option A — INCORRECT:} Converting all tables to 	extbf{DirectQuery} would provide live data, but the problem explicitly states that full DirectQuery performance on the large dimension tables is unacceptable. This approach sacrifices the performance advantage of in-memory caching without any benefit for static data.

	extbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} A 15-minute scheduled refresh still operates in 	extbf{Import mode}, meaning inventory data will always be stale by up to 15 minutes. This violates the requirement that inventory must 	extit{always reflect the latest stock levels}.

	extbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} 	extbf{Dual storage mode} is designed for tables that benefit from acting as both Import and DirectQuery depending on the query context — it is typically applied to 	extit{dimension} tables in a Composite Model to avoid cross-source groupby queries, not to the fact table requiring real-time data. Applying Dual mode to the inventory table would still cache data locally, undermining the real-time requirement.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Composite Models – Mixing Import & DirectQuery

options.A: Set both Source A and Source B to \textbf{Dual} mode. Power BI will intelligently route queries to the in-memory cache for performance while automatically falling back to DirectQuery when real-time results are required.
options.B: Set Source A to \textbf{DirectQuery} mode and Source B to \textbf{Import} mode. Connect them via standard relationships to enable cross-filtering while ensuring Source A queries always access current transactional data.
options.C: Import both sources using \textbf{incremental refresh} on Source A with 15-minute refresh intervals. Configure automatic page refresh to update visuals every 30 seconds, simulating real-time behavior.
options.D: Set Source A to \textbf{Import} mode with \textbf{automatic page refresh} enabled every 2 seconds, and Source B to \textbf{DirectQuery} mode to minimize storage on the larger dataset.

explanation:
\textbf{Requirement Analysis:}

The scenario specifies four critical constraints:
\begin{itemize}
\item Real-time query results from Source A (156M continuously-updated rows)
\item Minimal import storage overhead
\item Cross-source filtering capability
\item Sub-second performance on reference data
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Statement A — FALSE:} \textbf{Dual mode does NOT guarantee real-time results.} Dual mode stores data in both the in-memory cache and as DirectQuery. When a Dual table is queried alone or filtered within a context of other Import/Dual tables, Power BI prioritizes the cached (import) copy for performance. The table only falls back to DirectQuery when it is queried in the context of another DirectQuery table in the same model — this is a relationship/context-driven fallback, not a query-type-driven fallback. For a strictly real-time requirement on Source A, Dual mode is unreliable because it will often return stale cached data.

\textbf{Statement B — CORRECT:} This is the standard and recommended composite model pattern for this scenario:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Source A (DirectQuery):} Ensures every query against transactional data passes directly to the Azure SQL Database, guaranteeing current results. DirectQuery respects all real-time constraints and supports cross-filtering natively.

\item \textbf{Source B (Import):} The small 425 KB quarterly-updated file is imported entirely, consuming negligible storage (~1–2 MB in memory). Import mode provides sub-second query performance.

\item \textbf{Cross-filtering:} Standard relationships between DirectQuery and Import tables automatically enable bi-directional filtering in Power BI without requiring bridging tables or additional architectural complexity.
\end{itemize}

This combination meets all four requirements: real-time access (DirectQuery), minimized storage (import only the small reference), cross-filtering (native relationship support), and cached reference performance (Import mode).

\textbf{Statement C — FALSE:} \textbf{Incremental refresh is not applicable to DirectQuery tables and does not provide true real-time behavior.} Incremental refresh applies only to Import and Dual (import partition) tables and operates on scheduled intervals (e.g., 15 minutes). Automatic page refresh (30 seconds) refreshes only the visual display; the underlying data in Source A would still be 15 minutes stale between incremental refresh cycles. The requirement states real-time filtering, not periodic updates. Additionally, importing 156 million rows violates the storage minimization requirement.

\textbf{Statement D — FALSE:} Placing the large transactional table (156M rows) in Import mode contradicts the minimize-storage requirement. Caching 156 million rows would consume 3–5 GB of Power BI Premium capacity. Conversely, placing the small reference dataset in DirectQuery mode is wasteful (adds unnecessary query latency to a static 425 KB file) and violates best practice. Automatic page refresh on Import data does not guarantee real-time transactional queries.

\textbf{Best Practice:} For composite models mixing large transactional (real-time, frequently-updated) and small reference (static, infrequently-updated) sources, use \textbf{DirectQuery for transactional tables} and \textbf{Import for reference tables}. This ensures real-time accuracy on volatile data while maximizing performance and minimizing storage on stable reference data.

text: A 	extbf{Power BI} developer is building a report for a retail organisation. The model contains two tables: \textbf{Sales} (sourced via 	extbf{DirectQuery} from 	extbf{Azure Synapse Analytics}) and \textbf{Product} (imported from a 	extbf{SharePoint} list). The developer needs to create a measure that calculates the 	extit{year-to-date} revenue filtered by a product category slicer. During testing, the developer notices that the slicer interaction causes unexpectedly slow performance. Which of the following is the 	extbf{most likely root cause} of the performance issue, and what is the 	extbf{recommended mitigation}?

options.A: The 	extbf{imported} 	extbf{Product} table cannot participate in relationships with a 	extbf{DirectQuery} table in a 	extbf{composite model}; the fix is to convert both tables to 	extbf{Import} mode.
options.B: When a slicer on the 	extbf{imported} 	extbf{Product} table filters the 	extbf{DirectQuery} 	extbf{Sales} table, 	extbf{Power BI} must issue a 	extit{cross-source} query that passes filter values to the remote source on every interaction; setting the 	extbf{Product} table's storage mode to 	extbf{Dual} allows 	extbf{Power BI} to satisfy the filter locally when possible, reducing round-trips to 	extbf{Azure Synapse Analytics}.
options.C: The performance issue is caused by the 	extbf{SharePoint} connector not supporting 	extbf{query folding}; the fix is to replace the 	extbf{SharePoint} source with a 	extbf{SQL Server} source so that filter predicates can be pushed down.
options.D: Setting the 	extbf{Sales} table to 	extbf{Import} mode and keeping 	extbf{Product} in 	extbf{DirectQuery} mode will resolve the issue because 	extbf{DirectQuery} tables always benefit from in-memory caching when paired with an imported fact table.

explanation:
\textbf{Option B — CORRECT:} In a 	extbf{composite model}, when a slicer on an 	extbf{Import}-mode table (\textbf{Product}) filters a 	extbf{DirectQuery}-mode table (\textbf{Sales}), 	extbf{Power BI} must translate the selected filter values into a query sent to the remote source (\textbf{Azure Synapse Analytics}) on every slicer change. This 	extit{cross-source filtering} introduces latency proportional to the number of distinct values and the round-trip time to the remote source. The recommended mitigation is to set the 	extbf{Product} table's storage mode to \textbf{Dual}. A 	extbf{Dual}-mode table is cached in memory 	extit{and} retains its DirectQuery capability, allowing 	extbf{Power BI}'s query engine to resolve the filter locally against the in-memory cache instead of issuing additional remote queries, significantly improving slicer responsiveness.

\textbf{Option A — FALSE:} 	extbf{Composite models} explicitly support relationships between 	extbf{Import} and 	extbf{DirectQuery} tables; this is a core feature, not a limitation.

\textbf{Option C — FALSE:} While 	extbf{query folding} is relevant for transformation efficiency during data refresh, it does not govern real-time slicer interaction performance in a published report. This is a distractor mixing two separate concepts.

\textbf{Option D — FALSE:} Switching the 	extbf{Sales} (fact) table to 	extbf{Import} mode would change the data refresh semantics entirely and is not a targeted mitigation. Furthermore, 	extbf{DirectQuery} tables do not receive in-memory caching benefits simply by being paired with an imported table.
