text:
You are configuring an \textbf{incremental refresh} policy for a sales transaction table in Power BI Premium. The table contains \textit{OrderDate}, \textit{OrderID}, \textit{Amount}, and \textit{Region} columns. Your policy is configured as follows:

\begin{itemize}
\item Store the last 28 days of data in the model
\item Incrementally refresh the last 10 days of data
\item Use \textbf{RangeStart} and \textbf{RangeEnd} parameters for query folding
\end{itemize}

You configure the parameters correctly in the Advanced Editor:
\begin{verbatim}
let
  RangeStart = Date.AddDays(DateTime.FixedLocalNow(), -28),
  RangeEnd = DateTime.FixedLocalNow()
in
  ... filter [OrderDate] >= RangeStart and [OrderDate] <= RangeEnd
\end{verbatim}

During a scheduled refresh on March 15, the incremental refresh executes successfully. However, you later discover that the source system only contains transaction data back to March 6 due to a scheduled database maintenance window that archived older records.

What is the CORRECT interpretation of what will occur on the next refresh cycle, and what is the most appropriate response?

options.A: \textbf{RangeStart} will calculate to March 15 − 28 days = February 15, but the query will return no rows for dates before March 6. The incremental refresh will partially fail. You should reduce the storage window policy to 9 days to match available source data.
options.B: The \textbf{RangeStart} and \textbf{RangeEnd} parameters are calculated and applied correctly. Since the source contains no data matching the range before March 6, Power BI will load only the available records (March 6 onwards) into the respective partitions. No action is required unless query performance degrades.
options.C: The refresh will fail because \textbf{RangeStart} references a date (February 15) before the oldest available source data (March 6). You must contact the source system owner to restore the archived data, or temporarily adjust the storage window to align with current data availability.
options.D: Power BI will automatically detect the data gap and shift \textbf{RangeStart} forward to March 6 internally. The incremental refresh policy will continue operating without intervention, though historical trend analysis may be affected.

metadata.subject: Prepare the Data
metadata.sub_topic: Incremental Refresh – Range Parameters (RangeStart, RangeEnd), Policies

text: Your organization maintains a sales transaction dataset in 	extbf{Azure SQL Database} with over 7 million historical records. You are designing a 	extbf{Power BI} solution to refresh transaction data daily while minimizing query load and import time. You plan to implement 	extbf{incremental refresh} with range parameters. New transactions arrive continuously, and your refresh policy must capture only transactions from the last 11 days plus any modifications to existing records from the previous 85 days. You configure 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters in 	extbf{Power Query} as Date/DateTime parameters. However, during initial testing, you notice that the refresh is importing all historical data instead of applying the range filters. You verify that the parameters are defined in Power Query but the filter is not being pushed down to the SQL source. Which combination of actions will resolve this issue while maintaining the intended refresh strategy?

options.A: In 	extbf{Power Query Editor}, add a filter step that uses 	extbf{Table.SelectRows} to filter the date column where the value is between 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd}, configure incremental refresh on the table with 11 days incremental retention and 85 days full retention policy, and verify that the data source supports query folding.
options.B: Replace 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters with a hardcoded date calculation in the M formula using 	extbf{Date.AddDays()}, disable incremental refresh policy, and set a 11-day sliding window in a custom column instead.
options.C: Add a visual-level filter in the report to compare the date column against the parameters, apply the refresh policy to the entire dataset rather than the table, and ensure the 	extbf{Include data from today} toggle is disabled.
options.D: Configure the refresh policy without any Power Query date filters, rely on Power BI's automatic parameter injection at the dataset level, and apply the 11-day incremental and 85-day full retention periods through the Advanced refresh settings only.

explanation:
extbf{Statement 1 — Power Query Filter Using RangeStart and RangeEnd:} The core issue is that the 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters must be explicitly referenced in a 	extbf{Power Query filter step}. In the 	extbf{Power Query Editor}, you must add a step using 	extbf{Table.SelectRows} (or use the filter UI to create a step) that filters the date column with a condition such as the date being greater than or equal to 	extbf{RangeStart} and less than or equal to 	extbf{RangeEnd}. This filter step is the mechanism through which Power BI can perform query folding — pushing the predicate down to the SQL source engine at refresh time. Without this explicit filter step, the parameters are defined but unused, and all historical rows are retrieved.
	extbf{Statement 2 — Configure Incremental Refresh Policy:} After the Power Query filter is in place, you must enable incremental refresh on the 	extbf{table} (not the dataset) in 	extbf{Power BI Desktop} and set the retention policy: 11 days for incremental retention (new/modified rows) and 85 days for full refresh (complete reload cycle). This policy instructs Power BI which rows to keep in the semantic model and when to reload the full window.
	extbf{Statement 3 — Verify Query Folding Support:} The data source must support query folding so that the Power Query filter step can be translated into native SQL (SELECT...WHERE...) and executed on the source server. If query folding is not supported, the entire table is fetched and filtered in-memory in Power Query, defeating the purpose of incremental refresh. Verifying query folding capability prevents performance degradation.
	extbf{Why other options are incorrect:} Option B replaces dynamic parameters with hardcoded date calculations, eliminating the range parameter mechanism entirely and breaking the refresh automation. Option C applies filters at the report visual level, which does not reduce data import and does not trigger incremental refresh logic at the source. Option D removes Power Query filters completely and incorrectly assumes that incremental refresh policy alone (without an explicit range filter in Power Query) will constrain the source query — this is false; the filter step is mandatory for query folding to occur.
	extbf{Best Practice:} Incremental refresh in Power BI requires: (1) 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters defined in Power Query, (2) an explicit filter step in Power Query referencing these parameters on the date column, (3) verification that query folding is supported by the connector, and (4) incremental refresh policy configured on the table in Power BI Desktop. This multi-step configuration ensures that only the necessary date range is fetched from the source, minimizing bandwidth and refresh time.

text:
A retail company has a 	extbf{Power BI Premium} workspace. Their 	extbf{Sales} table contains five years of historical transaction data (approximately 200 million rows). The data team wants to configure 	extbf{incremental refresh} so that only the most recent 	extbf{60 days} of data are refreshed on each scheduled run, while keeping the previous 	extbf{4 years} of data archived as historical partitions that are never refreshed again. A junior analyst proposes the following setup:\[6pt]
\begin{itemize}
  \item Create 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters in 	extbf{Power Query} with a 	extbf{Date/Time} data type.
  \item Filter the date column in the query using these parameters.
  \item In the incremental refresh policy, set the 	extbf{store rows in the following date range} to 5 years and the 	extbf{refresh rows in the following date range} to 60 days.
  \item Publish the report to a 	extbf{Pro} workspace to enable the policy.
\end{itemize}\[6pt]
Which part of the proposed setup will cause the incremental refresh policy to 	extbf{fail to activate}?

options.A: The 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters must use a 	extbf{Text} data type, not 	extbf{Date/Time}.
options.B: The refresh range of 60 days cannot be smaller than one quarter; it must be set to at least 90 days.
options.C: Incremental refresh policies require the dataset to be published to a 	extbf{Power BI Premium} (or Premium Per User) workspace, not a 	extbf{Pro} workspace.
options.D: The store range must always equal the refresh range; setting them to different durations (5 years vs. 60 days) is not supported.

explanation:
\textbf{Option C — CORRECT:} Incremental refresh policies are only activated and enforced when a dataset is published to a \textbf{Power BI Premium} capacity (P SKUs), \textbf{Premium Per User (PPU)}, or \textbf{Embedded (A SKUs)} workspace. Publishing to a \textbf{Power BI Pro} workspace means the policy definition is saved in the model, but it is \textit{never applied} — the entire table is refreshed on every run, defeating the purpose entirely.\\[6pt]
\textbf{Option A — FALSE:} The \textbf{RangeStart} and \textbf{RangeEnd} parameters must be of type \textbf{Date/Time} (or Date/Time/Timezone). Using a Text data type would prevent Power BI from recognising the parameters for partitioning and would cause errors.\\[6pt]
\textbf{Option B — FALSE:} There is no minimum refresh range of 90 days enforced by Power BI. The refresh range can be set in days, and values such as 60 days are fully supported. The refresh window must simply be smaller than or equal to the store window.\\[6pt]
\textbf{Option D — FALSE:} The entire design principle of incremental refresh is that the \textbf{store range} (total data retained) is larger than the \textbf{refresh range} (data refreshed each cycle). Historical partitions outside the refresh range are frozen and not re-queried, which is the primary performance benefit of this feature.

text: A retail company stores transactional data in 	extbf{Azure SQL Database}. The 	extbf{Power BI} dataset uses 	extbf{incremental refresh} on a 	extbf{Sales} table. The current policy is configured as follows: 	extit{store rows from the past 3 years} and 	extit{refresh rows from the past 14 days}. The dataset was first published in January 2022 and today's date is June 2025. A data engineer notices that the historical partitions from 2022 are being re-refreshed every time a scheduled refresh runs, causing excessive source query load. After investigation, they discover that no 	extbf{RangeStart} or 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters exist in the Power Query editor for the 	extbf{Sales} table. Which of the following statements BEST explains the root cause and the correct remediation?

options.A: The incremental refresh policy requires that 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters be present in 	extbf{Power Query} with exactly those names, typed as 	extbf{Date/Time}, and applied as filters on the date column. Without these parameters, Power BI cannot partition the table and falls back to full table refresh on every cycle. The fix is to add both parameters and apply them as query filters before republishing.
options.B: Historical partitions are re-refreshed because the 	extbf{detect data changes} option is enabled. Disabling it in the incremental refresh policy settings will prevent historical partitions from being re-queried.
options.C: The root cause is that the 	extbf{Sales} table lacks a 	extbf{DateTime} column marked as the sort key. Adding a sort key in the model view will allow Power BI to isolate partitions and stop refreshing historical data.
options.D: The issue occurs because the dataset was published before the incremental refresh policy was defined. Deleting and republishing the dataset from scratch will automatically create the required internal partitions and stop historical data from being re-refreshed.

explanation:
extbf{Option A — CORRECT:} 	extbf{Incremental refresh} in Power BI has a strict prerequisite: two 	extbf{Power Query} parameters named exactly 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} (case-sensitive) must exist and must be of type 	extbf{Date/Time}. These parameters must be used to filter the date/time column in the query so that Power BI can fold the partition boundaries down to the data source and create isolated partitions. If these parameters are missing, Power BI cannot generate partition-level queries and performs a complete table refresh on every cycle — exactly the symptom described. The correct remediation is to create both parameters, apply them as filters on the relevant date column using a native query-foldable step, then re-publish the dataset so Power BI can build and maintain the partition structure correctly.

	extbf{Option B — INCORRECT:} The 	extit{detect data changes} feature is an optional advanced setting that allows a specific column to be checked for changes in historical partitions. Even if enabled, it only triggers selective historical refresh when changes are detected — it is not the root cause when 	extbf{RangeStart}/	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters are absent, and disabling it would not resolve a missing-parameter scenario.

	extbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} There is no concept of a ''sort key'' in Power BI model view that drives incremental refresh partitioning. Partitioning is governed exclusively by the 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters in Power Query and the policy defined in 	extbf{Power BI Desktop}.

	extbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} While republishing is sometimes necessary after making incremental refresh changes, simply deleting and republishing without adding the required 	extbf{RangeStart}/	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters will not resolve the issue. The parameters and their application as query filters are mandatory prerequisites regardless of when the dataset was first published.

text:
You are configuring 	extbf{incremental refresh} for a Power BI dataset that ingests 56 million customer transaction records from a cloud data warehouse spanning 2019 to present. Your organization requires:

• Daily refresh of only the last 5 days of data
• Historical data retention of 19 months
• Zero query folding failures during incremental window evaluation
• Minimal source system query execution time

You have defined 	extbf{RangeStart} and 	extbf{RangeEnd} parameters in Power Query. The incremental refresh policy is configured to:

• Store rows in the last: 19 months
• Incrementally refresh rows in the last: 5 days

During testing, you observe that Power BI is querying the entire 19-month historical window instead of only the 5-day incremental window. The query fold indicator shows that \textbf{RangeStart} and \textbf{RangeEnd} filters are not being pushed down to the source system. Which combination of actions would MOST effectively resolve this issue while maintaining business requirements?

options.A: Increase the incremental refresh window to 31 days, disable query folding entirely, and configure a separate Power BI dataflow to handle historical data retention separately from the main dataset.
options.B: Verify that the partition column (typically the transaction date) contains no NULL values, ensure \textbf{RangeStart} and \textbf{RangeEnd} filters are applied in the earliest stage of the Power Query M code before any transformations, and confirm the source connector supports native query folding for the date filter expression.
options.C: Reduce the historical retention window to 5 days to match the incremental window, remove all custom M transformations after the source query, and manually refresh the dataset twice daily to force partition metadata alignment.
options.D: Change the partition column from a date data type to a text-based timestamp, increase the 'Store rows' setting to 31 months, and create a separate incremental refresh policy for rows older than 19 months.

text: You are configuring a \textbf{dataflow} in Power BI Premium to support self-service ETL operations. You need to create a table that depends on another table already defined in the same dataflow, applying a filter and aggregation transformation. Which type of table should you create to reference the existing table without duplicating the source data?

options.A: A \textbf{Computed Table} that references the existing table and applies the required transformations
options.B: A \textbf{Linked Table} that establishes a direct connection to the original data source
options.C: A \textbf{Staging Table} that copies all data from the existing table before applying filters
options.D: A \textbf{Source Query} that re-executes the original data transformation logic

explanation:
\textbf{Statement A — TRUE:} A \textbf{Computed Table} in Power BI dataflows is designed specifically for scenarios where you need to reference another table already defined within the same dataflow and apply transformations (filtering, aggregation, joins) to it. Computed Tables avoid data duplication by operating on the processed result of the referenced table.

\textbf{Statement B — FALSE:} A \textbf{Linked Table} creates a connection to a table output from another dataflow or from an external data source. While linked tables are useful for reusing dataflow outputs across multiple dataflows, they do not apply transformations within the current dataflow context.

\textbf{Statement C — FALSE:} Staging tables are not a formal feature category in Power BI dataflows. Creating a copy of data would violate the principle of efficiency and would not be the recommended approach for referencing existing dataflow tables.

\textbf{Statement D — FALSE:} A Source Query typically refers to the initial connection to external data sources (databases, APIs, files). Re-executing the original logic would create redundancy and is not the intended method for referencing tables within the same dataflow.

\textbf{Key Concept:} In Power BI Premium dataflows, \textit{Computed Tables} enable self-service ETL by allowing analysts to build derived tables that depend on earlier transformations within the same dataflow, promoting data reuse and transformation efficiency.

text: You are configuring a \textbf{dataflow} in Power BI Premium to consolidate customer data from multiple sources. You create a \textbf{linked table} that references an existing \textbf{computed table} from another dataflow. During development, you notice that changes to the upstream computed table are not immediately reflected in your linked table. What is the most likely reason for this behavior?

options.A: The upstream dataflow containing the source computed table has not yet completed its refresh cycle; linked tables display cached data until the source dataflow refreshes.
options.B: Linked tables in Power BI always require a full reimport of the source dataflow schema to detect any data changes.
options.C: Computed tables cannot be used as sources for linked tables; only native data source tables from connectors are supported.
options.D: The dataflow entity settings have blocked downstream refresh propagation, which prevents automatic refresh dependency ordering.

explanation:
\textbf{Statement A — TRUE:} In Power BI Premium dataflows, \textbf{linked tables} establish dependencies on upstream \textbf{computed tables}. A linked table references the output of a computed table, which may reside in the same dataflow or a different dataflow. When data in the source computed table changes, those changes are only visible in the linked table after the source dataflow has completed its refresh cycle — whether that refresh is scheduled or manual. The linked table will display cached data from the previous refresh of the source until a new refresh of the source dataflow is initiated and completed. This dependency ordering is managed automatically by Power BI, but changes are not reflected in real-time; they propagate only after the upstream refresh completes.

\textbf{Statement B — FALSE:} Linked tables are designed to avoid constant full reimports. The linked table maintains a reference to the computed table output and refreshes incrementally. Schema detection occurs on initial creation, not on every refresh cycle.

\textbf{Statement C — FALSE:} Computed tables are explicitly supported as sources for linked tables. This is a core feature of Power BI Premium dataflows, enabling modular and reusable self-service ETL logic across multiple dataflows.

\textbf{Statement D — FALSE:} Power BI automatically manages refresh dependency ordering for linked tables across dataflows. There is no entity-level blocking setting that prevents downstream refresh propagation. The system ensures the source dataflow completes before the dependent dataflow attempts to refresh its linked table.

text: You are designing a data ingestion and transformation strategy for your organization using \textbf{Power BI Dataflows}. Your requirement is to extract customer data from an Azure SQL database into a dataflow, then apply data quality transformations (removing duplicates, standardizing formats) on that ingested data, and make the cleaned result available for reuse across multiple datasets. Your organization has \textbf{Power BI Premium} capacity. Which two-step approach should you use?

options.A: Create a \textbf{standard entity} that connects to Azure SQL to ingest raw customer data, then create a \textbf{Computed Table} that applies the transformations on that ingested entity.
options.B: Create a \textbf{Linked Table} that references an external Azure SQL source directly, applying transformations within the linked table definition.
options.C: Create a \textbf{Computed Table} that directly connects to Azure SQL, ingests the data, and applies transformations in a single step.
options.D: Create a standard dataflow entity that connects to Azure SQL, then reference it using a \textbf{Linked Table} from another workspace to apply transformations.

explanation:
extbf{Statement 1 — Correct Approach:} The correct answer is 	extbf{A}. In 	extbf{Power BI Dataflows}, data ingestion and transformation follow a two-step process. First, you create a 	extbf{standard entity} that connects to the external data source (Azure SQL database) and loads the raw data into the dataflow. Second, you create a 	extbf{Computed Table} that references this ingested entity and applies transformations (removing duplicates, standardizing formats). The Computed Table stores the processed result, enabling reuse across multiple datasets without re-running transformation logic.

	extbf{Statement 2 — Key Definition of Computed Table:} 	extbf{Computed Tables do not connect directly to external sources.} A Computed Table transforms and processes data that already exists within the dataflow (from a standard entity or linked table). It is a derived table based on Power Query transformations applied to internal dataflow entities. This distinction is critical: standard entities ingest external data; Computed Tables transform data already within the dataflow.

	extbf{Statement 3 — Why B is Incorrect:} 	extbf{Linked Tables} cannot connect directly to external sources like Azure SQL databases. A Linked Table references a table from another dataflow (which may be in the same workspace or across workspaces within the same tenant, depending on Premium configuration). They do not perform external connections or ingestion.

	extbf{Statement 4 — Why C is Incorrect:} As explained above, 	extbf{Computed Tables do not directly connect to external sources.} A Computed Table cannot ingest data directly from Azure SQL. The correct workflow requires a standard entity to ingest first, then a Computed Table to transform the ingested data within the dataflow context.

	extbf{Statement 5 — Why D is Incorrect:} While a standard entity can connect to Azure SQL, using a Linked Table to reference it does not apply transformations. Linked Tables simply reference existing dataflow tables without modification. Transformation requires a Computed Table, not a Linked Table.

	extbf{Key Concepts:}
	extbf{Standard Entity:} Connects to external data sources (Azure SQL, cloud storage, APIs) and loads raw data into the dataflow.
	extbf{Computed Table:} References tables already within the dataflow and applies 	extit{Power Query} transformations. It stores processed results and requires 	extbf{Power BI Premium} capacity.
	extbf{Linked Table:} References a table from another dataflow (same workspace or across workspaces) for reuse, without transformation capability.

text: A data engineer has created a \textbf{dataflow} in \textbf{Power BI Premium} that performs a complex set of transformations on raw sales data, including joins, aggregations, and cleansing steps. A second dataflow needs to use the already-transformed output of the first dataflow as its input, without re-executing the transformation logic. Which type of table should the second dataflow use to reference the first dataflow's output?

options.A: A \textbf{linked table}, which references an entity from another dataflow and re-uses its data without re-running the source transformations.
options.B: A \textbf{computed table}, which reads from a dataflow entity stored in \textbf{Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2} and applies additional transformations on top of it.
options.C: A \textbf{standard table}, which ingests data directly from the original source system by duplicating the connection credentials.
options.D: An \textbf{import table}, which caches a snapshot of the first dataflow's entities into the local \textbf{Power BI dataset} model.

explanation: \textbf{Option A — CORRECT:} A \textbf{linked table} in \textbf{Power BI Dataflows} allows one dataflow to reference an entity (table) produced by another dataflow directly, without re-executing the upstream transformation logic. This promotes reusability and a modular \textit{self-service ETL} pattern where analytical tables built downstream can consume curated data from upstream dataflows efficiently. Linked tables are available in \textbf{Power BI Premium} workspaces and enable the separation of data preparation concerns across teams.\\\textbf{Option B — INCORRECT:} A \textbf{computed table} is used within the \textit{same} dataflow to apply additional transformations on top of data already loaded into \textbf{Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2} (i.e., referencing another table within the same dataflow). Computed tables require a \textbf{Power BI Premium} capacity and are optimised for in-dataflow transformation layering, not cross-dataflow referencing.\\\textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} A \textbf{standard table} connects to an original data source directly. It does not reference entities from other dataflows and would require duplicating the full ETL logic, defeating the purpose of modular dataflow design.\\\textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} There is no \textbf{import table} concept within \textbf{Power BI Dataflows}. Import mode is a dataset storage concept that applies to \textbf{Power BI datasets}, not to dataflow entity types.

text:
A data analyst at a multinational company is designing a \textbf{Power BI dataflow} architecture in a \textbf{Premium per Capacity} workspace. The company has a master \textbf{Customer} table sourced from an on-premises SQL Server database containing \textbf{370,000} records. Three other business units each need to consume this Customer table in their own dataflows and apply unit-specific transformations (filtering by region, adding calculated columns, etc.) without duplicating the original data extraction.

The analyst wants to ensure that:
\begin{itemize}
\item The SQL Server source is queried only once during refresh.
\item Each business unit can independently apply its own transformations.
\item Changes to the master extraction logic propagate automatically to all downstream dataflows.
\end{itemize}

Which approach should the analyst implement?

options.A: Create a base dataflow with the \textbf{Customer} table, then create \textbf{linked tables} in each business unit's dataflow that reference the base table, followed by \textbf{computed tables} that apply unit-specific transformations to the linked tables.
options.B: Create a single dataflow containing \textbf{four copies} of the Customer table, each with different transformation logic applied, and schedule them all to refresh sequentially.
options.C: Create a base dataflow with the \textbf{Customer} table, then use \textbf{DirectQuery} connections from each business unit's dataflow to query the base dataflow in real time.
options.D: Create separate dataflows in each business unit workspace, each independently connecting to the on-premises SQL Server using its own \textbf{data gateway}, and apply unit-specific transformations within each dataflow.

explanation:
\textbf{Option A — CORRECT:} This is the recommended pattern for \textbf{Premium dataflows} that leverages the \textit{linked and computed table} architecture:

\begin{itemize}
\item A \textbf{linked table} is a read-only reference to a table in another dataflow. It does \textit{not} re-extract data from the original source — it references the already-ingested data stored in \textbf{Azure Data Lake Storage} (the enhanced compute engine in Premium). This satisfies the requirement that SQL Server is queried only once.
\item A \textbf{computed table} is created by applying transformations (filters, merges, calculated columns) to a linked table entirely within the dataflow engine — no additional source queries are needed.
\item When the base dataflow refreshes, all downstream linked tables automatically reflect the updated data upon their next refresh, ensuring propagation of extraction logic changes.
\end{itemize}

This pattern cleanly separates the \textbf{extraction/staging layer} from the \textbf{transformation layer}, embodying \textit{self-service ETL} best practices.

\textbf{Option B — INCORRECT:} Four copies of the same table in one dataflow would query the SQL Server source four times and would not allow independent business unit ownership of transformations.

\textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} \textbf{DirectQuery} is not a connection mode available between dataflows. Dataflows produce snapshot data loaded into storage, not real-time query endpoints.

\textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} Having each business unit independently connect to SQL Server means the source is queried \textbf{three additional times} (once per unit), violating the single-query requirement. Changes to extraction logic would also need to be replicated manually across all dataflows.

text:
You are building a self-service ETL solution in Power BI Premium using \textbf{Dataflows}. Your organization has a source dataset containing 12 months of sales transactions. You need to create a staging layer where you perform data cleansing, add a calculated column that flags high-value transactions (sales > \$5,000), and then reference this cleaned data in multiple downstream dataflows without duplicating transformations.

Which combination of dataflow table types should you use to meet these requirements while optimizing compute resources and maintenance?

options.A: Create a \textbf{standard (ingestion) table} that pulls raw data from the source, then build a \textbf{Computed table} on top of it that applies cleansing logic and the calculated column. Reference the Computed table in downstream dataflows using \textbf{Linked tables}.
options.B: Create a \textbf{Computed table} directly against the source system with all cleansing and calculated columns, then create multiple independent \textbf{standard tables} that each re-import raw data for each downstream dataflow.
options.C: Create a \textbf{Linked table} pointing to the external source system to ingest raw data, apply all transformations inline on the Linked table, and duplicate this logic in each downstream dataflow.
options.D: Create two \textbf{Computed tables} in a single dataflow --- one for cleansing and one for the calculated column --- then re-create both Computed tables in every downstream dataflow that needs the high-value flag.

explanation:
\textbf{Statement 1 --- Standard (Ingestion) Table:} A \textbf{standard table} in a dataflow is used to connect to and ingest data from an external source (e.g., a database, file, or API). It pulls raw data into the dataflow without performing heavy transformations. This serves as the foundational ingestion layer.

\textbf{Statement 2 --- Computed Table:} A \textbf{Computed table} is built on top of another table \textit{within the same dataflow} (such as the standard ingestion table). It applies transformations --- including data cleansing and the calculated column flagging sales > \$5,000 --- and stores the transformed results in Azure Data Lake Storage. Because the compute is executed once and results are persisted, downstream consumers benefit without re-running the transformation logic.

\textbf{Statement 3 --- Linked Table:} A \textbf{Linked table} references a table from \textit{another dataflow within the same Power BI tenant}. Downstream dataflows can use Linked tables to consume the already-transformed Computed table without duplicating transformation logic or re-ingesting from the source. This follows the \textit{single source of truth} principle and conserves Premium capacity.

\textbf{Why B is incorrect:} Re-importing raw data independently in every downstream dataflow duplicates extraction overhead, increases source system load, and violates ETL best practices.

\textbf{Why C is incorrect:} A \textbf{Linked table} references a table in \textit{another dataflow}, not an external source system. Linked tables cannot be used to ingest data from external sources. Duplicating transformation logic across dataflows also increases maintenance burden.

\textbf{Why D is incorrect:} Re-creating Computed tables in every downstream dataflow defeats the purpose of a shared staging layer, wastes Premium capacity through redundant compute, and creates a maintenance problem when transformation logic needs updating.

text:
Your organization uses a shared \textbf{Power BI Premium} workspace to centralize data preparation for 47 business users across Finance and Operations departments. You need to build a \textbf{dataflow} that ingests raw sales transaction data from an Azure SQL database, applies 16 transformation steps (data type conversions, filtering, column renames), and outputs a cleaned table called \textit{Sales\_Clean}. Four downstream Power BI datasets currently depend on \textit{Sales\_Clean} as their source. Your manager requests that you create a second output table called \textit{Sales\_Summary} within the same dataflow that aggregates \textit{Sales\_Clean} by Region and Quarter. The Finance team will use \textit{Sales\_Summary} in a dedicated report, while Operations will continue to use \textit{Sales\_Clean} for detailed drill-downs. You want to minimize refresh overhead and avoid duplicating the 16 transformation steps.

Which combination of \textbf{standard query table} and \textbf{computed table} strategies should you implement?

options.A: Create \textit{Sales\_Clean} as a \textbf{standard query table} connecting directly to Azure SQL with all 16 transformations, then create \textit{Sales\_Summary} as a \textbf{computed table} that references \textit{Sales\_Clean}.
options.B: Create \textit{Sales\_Clean} as a \textbf{computed table} with all 16 transformations, then create \textit{Sales\_Summary} as a second \textbf{computed table} that aggregates \textit{Sales\_Clean}.
options.C: Create both \textit{Sales\_Clean} and \textit{Sales\_Summary} as \textbf{standard query tables} pointing directly to the Azure SQL database, applying separate WHERE clauses and aggregations for each.
options.D: Create \textit{Sales\_Clean} as a \textbf{computed table}, then store \textit{Sales\_Summary} in a separate dataflow as a \textbf{linked table} referencing \textit{Sales\_Clean} to distribute processing load.

explanation:
\textbf{Statement 1 — Correct Table Classifications:} In Power BI dataflows, a \textbf{standard query table} (also called a regular or standard entity) is a table that connects directly to an external data source such as Azure SQL, Excel, or an API. A \textbf{computed table} is defined as a table that sources from \textit{another entity already materialized within the same dataflow} — it cannot source directly from external connectors. This distinction is critical to the design of efficient dataflows.

\textbf{Statement 2 — Why Option A is Correct:} Option A correctly identifies that \textit{Sales\_Clean}, which ingests from Azure SQL with 16 transformation steps, must be a \textbf{standard query table}. Once \textit{Sales\_Clean} is materialized and cached within the dataflow (leveraging \textbf{Premium capacity}), \textit{Sales\_Summary} is properly defined as a \textbf{computed table} that references the cached \textit{Sales\_Clean} entity and applies aggregation logic (Region and Quarter grouping). This design reuses the intermediate cached result, eliminating redundant execution of the 16 base transformations and minimizing refresh overhead.

\textbf{Statement 3 — Why Option B Contains a Definitional Error:} Option B labels \textit{Sales\_Clean} as a \textbf{computed table}. This is incorrect by Microsoft's definition. A computed table requires its source to be another dataflow entity, not an external connector like Azure SQL. \textit{Sales\_Clean} directly connected to Azure SQL must be a standard query table. Although \textit{Sales\_Summary} as a computed table is correctly identified, the mislabeling of \textit{Sales\_Clean} makes this option factually imprecise and not aligned with dataflow architecture best practices.

\textbf{Statement 4 — Why Option C Duplicates Work:} Option C creates both tables as \textbf{standard query tables} pointing directly to Azure SQL with separate WHERE clauses and aggregations. While syntactically possible, this approach forces Azure SQL to execute the 16 base transformations twice (once for each table definition), increasing database load, refresh time, and compute overhead. It defeats the consolidation and caching benefits that \textbf{Premium dataflows} provide and violates \textit{self-service ETL} efficiency principles.

\textbf{Statement 5 — Why Option D Complicates Architecture:} Option D splits the design across two dataflows, storing \textit{Sales\_Summary} as a \textbf{linked table} in a separate dataflow referencing \textit{Sales\_Clean}. While \textbf{linked tables} can reference tables from other dataflows, this introduces unnecessary cross-dataflow dependencies, requires two independent refresh schedules, complicates troubleshooting and maintenance, and adds latency. Keeping both tables within a single dataflow is simpler and ensures unified refresh coordination.

\textbf{Best Practice:} Design a single \textbf{Premium dataflow} with a \textbf{standard query table} for the Azure SQL connection and base transformations (\textit{Sales\_Clean}), followed by a \textbf{computed table} (\textit{Sales\_Summary}) that references \textit{Sales\_Clean}. This maximizes transformation reuse through Premium caching, minimizes refresh overhead, and exemplifies efficient \textit{self-service ETL} consolidation.

text: Your organization uses 	extbf{Power BI Premium} and has built a centralized 	extbf{dataflow} containing a 	extbf{computed table} that applies complex revenue normalization logic on top of a 	extbf{linked table} sourced from a shared base dataflow. The data engineering team reports that when they refresh the base dataflow, the computed table in the downstream dataflow sometimes reflects stale data. A junior analyst suggests simply scheduling both dataflows to refresh at the same time. As the 	extbf{Power BI Data Analyst}, which explanation and remediation is most accurate?

options.A: Linked tables always cache data independently, so scheduling simultaneous refreshes guarantees consistency. No further action is required.
options.B: Computed tables depend on linked tables, which depend on the base dataflow. Simultaneous scheduling does not guarantee execution order. The correct remediation is to enable 	extbf{enhanced compute engine} and configure the base dataflow to refresh first, then trigger the downstream dataflow refresh sequentially using 	extbf{Power Automate} or scheduled offset times.
options.C: Computed tables in 	extbf{Power BI Premium} dataflows cannot reference linked tables across different dataflows; the data model must be restructured to use direct query sources instead.
options.D: The stale data issue is caused by the 	extbf{incremental refresh} policy on the base dataflow conflicting with the linked table. Disabling incremental refresh on the base dataflow will resolve the problem without any scheduling changes.

explanation:
\textbf{Option B — CORRECT:} In \textbf{Power BI Premium} dataflows, a \textbf{computed table} performs transformations on top of data already ingested into the dataflow storage layer, and a \textbf{linked table} references a table from another dataflow. Because linked tables read from the source dataflow's storage, if the downstream dataflow refreshes before or simultaneously with the base dataflow, it will consume the previous version of the data, causing staleness. The recommended remediation is to ensure the base dataflow refreshes first and then trigger the downstream dataflow, either by using \textbf{Power Automate} to chain refreshes via the Power BI REST API or by offsetting scheduled refresh times sufficiently. The \textbf{enhanced compute engine} should also be enabled to maximize computed table performance on Premium capacity.

\textbf{Option A — INCORRECT:} Simultaneous scheduling does not control execution order. There is no guarantee that the base dataflow completes before the downstream dataflow begins reading from the linked table.

\textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} Computed tables referencing linked tables across different workspaces and dataflows is a supported and intended feature of \textbf{Power BI Premium} dataflows. This cross-dataflow referencing pattern is precisely the \textit{self-service ETL} reuse architecture promoted by Microsoft.

\textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} While incremental refresh can affect which partitions are updated, it is not the root cause of this staleness issue. Disabling incremental refresh would not fix the execution-order dependency between the base and downstream dataflows.

text:
A large enterprise has implemented a 	extbf{Power BI Premium} workspace. The data engineering team has created a 	extbf{dataflow} containing a complex set of transformations on raw sales data — including currency conversions, deduplication logic, and multi-step aggregations — that takes approximately 45 minutes to refresh. Multiple downstream analytical teams each need to apply their own 	extit{department-specific} filters and joins on top of this cleansed dataset without re-executing the expensive base transformations.

Which combination of 	extbf{dataflow table types} should the data engineering team implement to achieve this goal most efficiently?

options.A: Create one 	extbf{standard table} for the base transformations and instruct each downstream team to connect directly to the source and re-apply the base transformations independently in their own dataflows.
options.B: Create one 	extbf{computed table} for the base transformations, then have each downstream team create 	extbf{linked tables} referencing that computed table and apply their department-specific logic on top.
options.C: Create one 	extbf{linked table} for the base transformations, then have each downstream team create 	extbf{computed tables} from the original source, bypassing the shared cleansing logic.
options.D: Create a single 	extbf{standard table} that includes all possible department-specific filters and publish it as one shared output for all teams to consume without further transformation.

explanation:
\textbf{Option B is correct.} In \textbf{Power BI Premium} dataflows, a \textbf{computed table} stores the results of an in-dataflow transformation into the \textit{Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2} backing store. Because the output is persisted, downstream consumers do not need to re-execute the expensive upstream transformations during their own refresh cycles. A \textbf{linked table} references an existing entity from another dataflow (or the same dataflow) and allows consumers to apply additional transformations — such as department-specific filters and joins — on top of the already-materialized data. This architecture (computed table for the shared, expensive base layer + linked tables for consumer-specific logic) is the canonical \textit{self-service ETL} pattern recommended by Microsoft for Premium dataflows, minimising redundant compute and refresh time.

\textbf{Option A} is incorrect because it forces every team to independently re-execute the 45-minute base transformation, wasting compute and defeating the purpose of a shared dataflow layer.

\textbf{Option C} is incorrect because \textbf{linked tables} cannot themselves perform the initial costly transformation — they reference already-existing entities. Using computed tables downstream that bypass the shared logic re-introduces the redundant work. The architecture described inverts the correct relationship.

\textbf{Option D} is incorrect because embedding all department logic into a single standard table creates a maintenance burden, reduces flexibility, and still forces one large table to serve divergent needs without allowing downstream teams to apply their own transformations efficiently.

\textbf{Key distinction:} \textbf{Computed tables} require \textbf{Power BI Premium} (or Premium Per User) — they are not available in shared capacity. \textbf{Linked tables} are also a Premium feature when referencing tables across different dataflows.

text: Your organization has a 	extbf{Power BI Premium} workspace. A central 	extbf{dataflow} (	extbf{Dataflow A}) ingests raw sales transaction data from an on-premises SQL Server, applies currency conversion lookups, and outputs a cleansed table. A second dataflow (	extbf{Dataflow B}) needs to join the cleansed sales data with a product hierarchy table and pre-aggregate results by quarter for use in multiple downstream reports. The data engineering team requires that the aggregation logic runs only once and is physically stored, not re-executed on every refresh. Which table type should 	extbf{Dataflow B} use to consume the output of 	extbf{Dataflow A}, and what is the key reason for this choice?

options.A: A \textbf{linked table} referencing 	extbf{Dataflow A}'s output, because linked tables physically store a separate copy of the data and execute their own independent transformation pipeline on each refresh.
options.B: A \textbf{computed table} built on top of the linked entity from 	extbf{Dataflow A}, because computed tables leverage \textit{enhanced compute engine} to execute transformations in-storage once and serve pre-aggregated results without re-running the source query.
options.C: A \textbf{standard import table} inside 	extbf{Dataflow B} that re-queries the on-premises SQL Server directly, because this avoids a dependency chain between dataflows and ensures the freshest data.
options.D: A \textbf{linked table} referencing 	extbf{Dataflow A}'s output and marking it as \textit{DirectQuery mode}, because this prevents any data duplication across dataflows while still allowing live aggregations.

explanation: \textbf{Option B — CORRECT:} In \textbf{Power BI Premium}, dataflows support \textbf{computed tables}, which are created by applying additional transformations on top of a \textbf{linked entity} (a reference to a table from another dataflow). The critical distinction is that computed tables are physically materialized and stored in \textit{Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2} backing the Premium capacity. The \textbf{enhanced compute engine} executes the aggregation logic once during the dataflow refresh and stores the result, so downstream reports read the pre-computed output rather than re-triggering source queries. This pattern — a linked entity feeding a computed table — is the canonical \textit{self-service ETL} architecture for 	extbf{Premium dataflows}: one layer ingests and cleanses, a second layer enriches and aggregates without touching the source again.\\\textbf{Option A — INCORRECT:} \textbf{Linked tables} are a read-only pointer to a table in another dataflow; they do \textit{not} store a separate physical copy or run an independent transformation pipeline. They simply surface the already-refreshed data from the source dataflow. Adding transformation logic requires promoting the linked table to a computed table.\\\textbf{Option C — INCORRECT:} Re-querying the on-premises source directly defeats the purpose of the centralised cleansing layer in \textbf{Dataflow A}, duplicates ingestion effort, bypasses the currency conversion logic, and adds load to the on-premises system. It is the antithesis of the \textit{single source of truth} principle in a dataflow architecture.\\\textbf{Option D — INCORRECT:} \textbf{Linked tables} in dataflows operate in import mode during refresh — there is no DirectQuery mode for inter-dataflow references within the dataflow authoring experience. Applying DirectQuery applies to \textbf{datasets} connecting to dataflows, not to table types within dataflows themselves.
